annotations.label: Definition of √2

anchor.snippet: is the positive real number that, when multiplied by itself or squared, equals the number 2

annotations.note: √2 is `Real.sqrt 2`, where `Real.sqrt` is defined as the nonnegative inverse of squaring, satisfying `Real.sq_sqrt`.

annotations.label: √2 is algebraic, not transcendental

anchor.snippet: It is an algebraic number , and therefore not a transcendental number

annotations.note: `IsAlgebraic`/`Transcendental` are defined but no declaration specifically establishes that √2 is algebraic over ℚ.

annotations.label: √2 as diagonal of unit square

anchor.snippet: is the length of a diagonal across a square with sides of one unit of length

annotations.note: No declaration relates √2 to the diagonal of a unit square, though general Euclidean distance machinery exists.

annotations.label: Rational approximation 99/70

anchor.snippet: is sometimes used as a good rational approximation with a reasonably small denominator

annotations.note: This specific rational approximation of √2 is not formalized.

annotations.label: Babylonian sexagesimal approximation (YBC 7289)

anchor.snippet: which is accurate to about six decimal digits

annotations.note: This historical numeric approximation is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: which is accurate to about six decimal digits

annotations.label: Sulbasutras approximation

anchor.snippet: Increase the length [of the side] by its third and this third by its own fourth less the thirty-fourth part of that fourth

annotations.note: This historical approximation formula is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: Increase the length [of the side] by its third and this third by its own fourth less the thirty-fourth part of that fourth

annotations.label: Pell-number approximation sequence

anchor.snippet: is the seventh in a sequence of increasingly accurate approximations based on the sequence of Pell numbers

annotations.note: Pell's-equation solution theory is formalized, but the Pell-number approximation sequence to √2 is not a named result.

annotations.label: Pythagorean discovery of irrationality

anchor.snippet: discovered that the diagonal of a square is incommensurable with its side

annotations.note: Incommensurability of a square's diagonal and side is exactly the irrationality of √2, `irrational_sqrt_two`.

annotations.label: Ad quadratum doubling of a square

anchor.snippet: the diagonal of the original square is equal to the side of the resulting square

annotations.note: This geometric construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Babylonian method (Newton's method)

anchor.snippet: which is used as a basis in many computers and calculators

annotations.note: General Newton iteration `Polynomial.newtonMap` is formalized, but the specific Babylonian square-root iteration is not.

annotations.label: Quadratic convergence of iteration

anchor.snippet: Each iteration improves the approximation, roughly doubling the number of correct digits

annotations.note: No quadratic-convergence theorem for the real Newton/Babylonian square-root iteration is formalized.

annotations.label: Error of 99/70 approximation

anchor.snippet: differs from the correct value by less than

annotations.note: This specific error bound is not formalized.

annotations.label: Four-iteration Babylonian approximation

anchor.snippet: is too large by about

annotations.note: This specific iterated numeric approximation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Irrationality via infinite descent

anchor.snippet: One proof of the number's irrationality is the following proof by infinite descent

annotations.note: The statement is `irrational_sqrt_two`; Mathlib proves it via prime factorization rather than infinite descent.

annotations.label: Irrationality via reciprocals

anchor.snippet: This gives the desired contradiction

annotations.note: The proven statement is `irrational_sqrt_two`, though the reciprocal-based proof technique is not the one used in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Irrationality via unique factorization

anchor.snippet: each side has the same prime factorization by the fundamental theorem of arithmetic

annotations.note: This matches Mathlib's actual proof, which goes via `Nat.Prime.irrational_sqrt` and `not_isSquare` using unique factorization.

annotations.label: Irrationality via rational root theorem

anchor.section: Application of the rational root theorem
anchor.snippet: also follows from the rational root theorem

annotations.note: Irrationality is `irrational_sqrt_two`, and the rational root theorem itself is formalized as `num_dvd_of_is_root`/`den_dvd_of_is_root`.

annotations.label: Square roots of non-squares are irrational

anchor.section: Application of the rational root theorem
anchor.snippet: may be used to show that any square root of any natural number that is not a perfect square is irrational

annotations.note: `irrational_sqrt_natCast_iff : Irrational (√n) ↔ ¬IsSquare n` is exactly this statement.

annotations.label: Tennenbaum's geometric proof

anchor.snippet: A simple proof is attributed to Stanley Tennenbaum

annotations.note: The statement is `irrational_sqrt_two`; Tennenbaum's specific geometric proof is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Apostol's geometric proof

anchor.snippet: Tom M. Apostol made another geometric reductio ad absurdum argument showing that

annotations.note: The statement is `irrational_sqrt_two`; Apostol's specific geometric proof is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Constructive irrationality proof

anchor.snippet: we can obtain a constructively stronger statement by using a positive definition of

annotations.note: Classical irrationality `irrational_sqrt_two` is formalized, but the constructively stronger explicit-separation statement is not.

annotations.label: Irrationality via Pythagorean triples

anchor.snippet: This proof uses the following property of primitive Pythagorean triples

annotations.note: The proven statement is `irrational_sqrt_two`; this Pythagorean-triple proof route is not the one used in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lemma: hypotenuse of primitive triple is never even

anchor.snippet: then c is never even

annotations.note: The primitive-triple classification is formalized (giving z = m²+n² with one of m,n even), but the parity lemma 'hypotenuse is odd' is not a standalone declaration.

annotations.label: Two equal squares never sum to a square

anchor.snippet: two identical perfect squares can never be added to produce another perfect square

annotations.note: This is equivalent to √2's irrationality (no nonzero a,c with 2a²=c²) and follows from `irrational_sqrt_two`, but is not stated as its own declaration.

annotations.label: Reciprocal of √2 equals its half

anchor.snippet: which is equal to its half, is a widely used constant

annotations.note: The identity 1/√2 = √2/2 is derivable from `Real.sqrt_inv` and basic algebra but is not a named declaration.

annotations.label: Unit vector at 45°

anchor.snippet: the unit vector , which makes a 45° angle with the axes in a plane , has the coordinates

annotations.note: `Real.cos_pi_div_four`/`Real.sin_pi_div_four` give cos(π/4)=sin(π/4)=√2/2, the 45° unit-vector coordinates.

annotations.label: Self-referential property of √2

anchor.snippet: One interesting property of

annotations.note: This self-referential identity for √2 is not formalized.

annotations.label: √2 from copies of the imaginary unit

anchor.snippet: can also be expressed in terms of copies of the imaginary unit

annotations.note: This expression of √2 via the imaginary unit is not formalized.

annotations.label: Infinite tetration equal to its square

anchor.snippet: is also the only real number other than 1 whose infinite tetration (i.e., infinite exponential tower) is equal to its square

annotations.note: Infinite tetration / power towers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: √2 in Viète's formula for π

anchor.snippet: appears in Viète's formula for

annotations.note: The nested-radical sequence `sqrtTwoAddSeries` and `cos_pi_over_two_pow` (the basis of Viète's product) are formalized, but Viète's formula itself is not assembled.

annotations.label: √2 in trigonometric constants

anchor.snippet: appears in various trigonometric constants

annotations.note: Trig constants involving √2 are formalized, e.g. `cos_pi_div_four = √2/2` and `cos_pi_div_eight = √(2+√2)/2`.

annotations.label: Normality of √2 unknown

anchor.snippet: is a normal number , which is a stronger property than irrationality

annotations.note: Normal numbers are not formalized in Mathlib (and normality of √2 is an open problem).

annotations.label: Infinite products from cos/sin π/4

anchor.snippet: along with the infinite product representations for the sine and cosine , leads to products such as

annotations.note: Euler's sine product is formalized, but the specific √2 infinite products are not assembled.

annotations.label: Taylor series of cos π/4

anchor.snippet: The number can also be expressed by taking the Taylor series of a trigonometric function

annotations.note: The cosine power series `Real.hasSum_cos` is formalized, but its evaluation at π/4 to express √2 is not a named result.

annotations.label: Double-factorial Taylor series

anchor.snippet: with x = 1 and using the double factorial

annotations.note: The double factorial is formalized, but the √2 series using it is not.

annotations.label: Euler-transform accelerated series

anchor.snippet: The convergence of this series can be accelerated with an Euler transform

annotations.note: The Euler transform of this √2 series is not formalized.

annotations.label: BBP-type formula for √2 unknown

anchor.snippet: can be represented with a BBP-type formula

annotations.note: BBP-type formulas are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Egyptian-fraction series for √2

anchor.snippet: The number can be represented by an infinite series of Egyptian fractions

annotations.note: Egyptian-fraction representations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continued fraction of √2

anchor.snippet: The square root of two has the following continued fraction representation

annotations.note: Continued-fraction computation and convergence are formalized, but the periodic [1;2,2,…] expansion of √2 is not established.

annotations.label: Convergents described by Pell numbers

anchor.snippet: form a sequence of fractions that approximate the square root of two to increasing accuracy, and that are described by the Pell numbers

annotations.note: Pell's-equation solution theory is formalized, but the identification of √2 convergents with Pell numbers is not a named result.

annotations.label: Nested square expressions converging to √2

anchor.snippet: The following nested square expressions converge to

annotations.note: These specific nested-radical expressions converging to √2 are not formalized.

annotations.label: Lichtenberg paper-size ratio

anchor.snippet: could be folded in half and aligned with its shorter side to produce a sheet with exactly the same proportions as the original

annotations.note: The √2 paper-aspect-ratio property is not formalized.

annotations.label: √2 as tritone frequency ratio

anchor.snippet: is the frequency ratio of a tritone interval in twelve-tone equal temperament music

annotations.note: This musical application of √2 is not formalized.

annotations.label: √2 in photographic f-stops

anchor.snippet: forms the relationship of f-stops in photographic lenses

annotations.note: This photographic application of √2 is not formalized.

annotations.label: √2 scaling of grid-cell mesh size

anchor.snippet: increases by the squareroot of two from one area to the next

annotations.note: This grid-cell mesh-size application of √2 is not formalized.
